LinkedIn respects your privacy

LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

  • Top Content
  • People
  • Learning
  • Jobs
  • Games
Sign in Join now

Boost Response Rates by Applying Directly on Company Pages

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer
3w Edited
  • Report this post

Two months ago, I submitted 10 applications to test out the market. I got responses on 7 of those (meaning I was either offered an interview or rejected); 2 others had auto-reply messages that noted I would only hear back if the application was moving forward so I didn't expect responses on those. So 2 months later, only 1 application seems to be lost in the void. I see posts where people claim they only get responses on 5-10% of applications which is pretty shocking to me. And I am confident that this higher response rate came down to one single thing I did: applying directly on the company pages. Why does this increase response rates so dramatically? 1. It confirms the job is real. We hear a lot about fake jobs, but for the most part, companies aren't posting fake jobs. But they post a job on their job board, it gets scraped onto the countless job boards out there, and when the company fills the role, the job posting remains. Companies have no idea their jobs are posted on these boards and no way to remove them. Many of these job boards aren't really intended to help jobseekers - some are collecting data to sell to marketers, some are trying to get traffic to their websites where they run ads. Some are AI appliers or resume tools and once you try and "apply" for a job, you land in a sales funnel. In fact, some of these job boards post jobs on sites like LinkedIn and Indeed themselves just to funnel you to their platforms. 2. It ensures the company gets your application. Back when I worked in education, we tried out easy apply but we couldn't afford the integration to push apps to our ATS. That meant our team had to log into the job boards to collect apps. Now if a company has this sort of setup, they may not check those apps regularly. One example: I was consulting with a non-profit years ago. They had over 1000 apps sitting in Indeed that no one had looked at; that same role in their ATS had 9 apps. We interviewed 6 of the 9 direct applications before even beginning the process of sorting through the 1000 apps on Indeed. 3. It ensures that you put your best foot forward on an application. Many folks are using AI appliers or applying through job boards that funnel candidates through an AI applier that fills out applications. The issue here is that the application triggers fraud signals and many of these may be filtered out. Companies may assume they're fraudulent and not bother applying. Or large chunks of the app are missing. For example, one popular AI applier that I get ads for regularly submits apps without a resume or LinkedIn...we get tons of them, and there's simply nothing we can do when all we have is a name and an email address for an AI application service. I know it sounds simple, but it's how we were applying back in 2016, and turns out it still works pretty well in 2026, at least in terms of ensuring that your application is reviewed.

323 109 Comments
Like Comment
Holly Baker, M.S. 3w
  • Report this comment

This is actually a metric I've been tracking in my application spreadsheet this year. For me, applying directly through company websites has performed a little better than LinkedIn Easy Apply or Handshake, but it definitely hasn't been a magic fix. About 48% of my company website applications ended in ghosting/no response, compared to 66% of applications submitted through LinkedIn Easy Apply or Handshake. So based on my own data, I'd agree that company websites seem to improve the odds. I just don't know that they're the solution. There are a lot of other barriers in the hiring process that applicants never really get visibility into.

Like
Reply
13 Reactions 14 Reactions
Robert Ridley 3w
  • Report this comment

Bonnie Dilber how are you defining "response?" Do you mean, an acknowledgment that your application was received, or a response saying whether or not you are getting an interview?

Like
Reply
8 Reactions 9 Reactions
Jessica Johnson 3w
  • Report this comment

"And I am confident that this higher response rate came down to one single thing I did: applying directly on the company pages." I would respectfully disagree, Bonnie. Amongst other possibilities, if you put a truthful resume forward, you are a cream of the crop pick - something most cannot claim to be. Hence, the response rate for you will be experienced much differently. Additionally, not all JDs/apps are present on all corporate websites, despite them (sometimes) actually being real jobs - a shame, to be sure...but also a fact.

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Kristen Fife 3w
  • Report this comment

I know 90% of the applications I submit result in a confirmation email. I truly believe most of the complaints are from not receiving a *personalized* response.

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Mouna Mira Chagraoui 3w
  • Report this comment

Are you serious Bonnie Dilber ? People are smart enough to identify market trends and the immense insecurity in the labour market today. 💯

Like
Reply
4 Reactions 5 Reactions
Alex Alaniz 3w
  • Report this comment

Legit candidates tripping fraud signals is the tragedy for both sides. Once an app clears that bar though (real, complete, applied direct), does a candidate's verifiability still factor in at all for you? Or at that point are you basically taking the resume at face value and letting the interview sort it out?

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Penny Christensen, Ed.D. 3w
  • Report this comment

I am not sure the feelings I have reading this post are what is intended. I have been applying for 5+ months. These are appropriate jobs, directly to the company for which I am applying. Yet, I am not receiving any responses on well over 50% of these applications. That does not reflect well on those companies who do not acknowledge my application/provide status or notify when the position is filled. This frustrates me; I try not to take it personally. But it is disheartening when I see the position reposted as the first communication about a position I previously applied for. I am not posting this to chastise those employers. However, am I reading this post as the lack of their response is somehow my responsibility? Please don’t make changing jobs right now harder.

Like
Reply
9 Reactions 10 Reactions
Stevie Prugar 3w
  • Report this comment

After 1,264 applications, I’ve realized the biggest issue in today’s hiring market isn’t rejection rates, it’s non-processing rates. Out of 1,264 applications: ~450 direct rejections ~50 interview-stage rejections ~4% interview rate, which is actually above average in today’s market I’ve heavily optimized my process: recruiter-reviewed resume, ATS-focused formatting, and automation that scans my inbox weekly and updates a dashboard tracking every application outcome. I also ONLY apply to roles I am at least a 95% match for. What I’ve learned is this: Roughly 40% of jobs simply are not real hiring opportunities. Whether it’s ghost jobs, companies collecting resumes for future openings, firms wanting to appear like they’re growing, or platforms farming traffic/data, I genuinely believe 2 out of every 5 postings are not tied to active hiring. That alone likely explains the majority of the ~60% of applications that never receive any response at all. The remaining ~20%, in my opinion, comes down to hiring processes themselves. Between broken ATS integrations and inconsistent workflows, many applications likely never get meaningfully reviewed.

Like
Reply
8 Reactions 9 Reactions
Yoshika Lowe, M.Ed, CSPO 3w
  • Report this comment

I’d add to also apply only to jobs you are truly qualified for. This is not a market that rewards aspirational/reach applicants.

Like
Reply
10 Reactions 11 Reactions
Dutch DeVries 3w
  • Report this comment

You got lucky. Do 10 more. Do 10 each week for a month. Your sampling is statistically insignificant and not a valid case study. But yes… applying on the company websites directly will get you a better response, but it doesn’t “prove” the job is real. Many companies post ghost jobs just to appear as though they’re hiring. Perfect example would be the major companies that lay off thousands but still have “openings” for the jobs they just dumped.

Like
Reply

    3w
    • Report this post

    Bonnie ran a small experiment about getting an update after applying. I usually have the majority of employers get back to me at some point. But here is the reality: if you don't hear back, move on. https://lnkd.in/gSh4d8iA

    Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer

    Recruiting Leader @ Zapier | Former Educator | I’m a fan of transparency in recruiting, leveraging AI to make work more efficient and human, and workplaces that work for everyone.

    3w Edited

    Two months ago, I submitted 10 applications to test out the market. I got responses on 7 of those (meaning I was either offered an interview or rejected); 2 others had auto-reply messages that noted I would only hear back if the application was moving forward so I didn't expect responses on those. So 2 months later, only 1 application seems to be lost in the void. I see posts where people claim they only get responses on 5-10% of applications which is pretty shocking to me. And I am confident that this higher response rate came down to one single thing I did: applying directly on the company pages. Why does this increase response rates so dramatically? 1. It confirms the job is real. We hear a lot about fake jobs, but for the most part, companies aren't posting fake jobs. But they post a job on their job board, it gets scraped onto the countless job boards out there, and when the company fills the role, the job posting remains. Companies have no idea their jobs are posted on these boards and no way to remove them. Many of these job boards aren't really intended to help jobseekers - some are collecting data to sell to marketers, some are trying to get traffic to their websites where they run ads. Some are AI appliers or resume tools and once you try and "apply" for a job, you land in a sales funnel. In fact, some of these job boards post jobs on sites like LinkedIn and Indeed themselves just to funnel you to their platforms. 2. It ensures the company gets your application. Back when I worked in education, we tried out easy apply but we couldn't afford the integration to push apps to our ATS. That meant our team had to log into the job boards to collect apps. Now if a company has this sort of setup, they may not check those apps regularly. One example: I was consulting with a non-profit years ago. They had over 1000 apps sitting in Indeed that no one had looked at; that same role in their ATS had 9 apps. We interviewed 6 of the 9 direct applications before even beginning the process of sorting through the 1000 apps on Indeed. 3. It ensures that you put your best foot forward on an application. Many folks are using AI appliers or applying through job boards that funnel candidates through an AI applier that fills out applications. The issue here is that the application triggers fraud signals and many of these may be filtered out. Companies may assume they're fraudulent and not bother applying. Or large chunks of the app are missing. For example, one popular AI applier that I get ads for regularly submits apps without a resume or LinkedIn...we get tons of them, and there's simply nothing we can do when all we have is a name and an email address for an AI application service. I know it sounds simple, but it's how we were applying back in 2016, and turns out it still works pretty well in 2026, at least in terms of ensuring that your application is reviewed.

    25 2 Comments

  • Danielle Antes
    3w
    • Report this post

    This post is a fabulous education on how applications are typically handled by recruiting teams. The post also makes a great point about fake jobs. I've only come across this once in my recruiting career, but since digging back in I've been shocked at how much content there is now around this topic (and the reverse - fake candidates 😲). We have to be smart connoisseurs of content, and job postings are no exception. Treat everything with the same level of skepticism until you can verify for yourself from a trusted, legitimate source. For job postings, a company website is your best bet. And FWIW - even if it's not the one you're hoping for, you'll *always* get a response from our team.

    Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer

    Recruiting Leader @ Zapier | Former Educator | I’m a fan of transparency in recruiting, leveraging AI to make work more efficient and human, and workplaces that work for everyone.

    3w Edited

    Two months ago, I submitted 10 applications to test out the market. I got responses on 7 of those (meaning I was either offered an interview or rejected); 2 others had auto-reply messages that noted I would only hear back if the application was moving forward so I didn't expect responses on those. So 2 months later, only 1 application seems to be lost in the void. I see posts where people claim they only get responses on 5-10% of applications which is pretty shocking to me. And I am confident that this higher response rate came down to one single thing I did: applying directly on the company pages. Why does this increase response rates so dramatically? 1. It confirms the job is real. We hear a lot about fake jobs, but for the most part, companies aren't posting fake jobs. But they post a job on their job board, it gets scraped onto the countless job boards out there, and when the company fills the role, the job posting remains. Many of these job boards aren't really intended to help jobseekers - some are collecting data to sell to marketers, some are trying to get traffic to their websites where they run ads.     Like Comment

  • Rakesh Meda
    4w
    • Report this post

    Stop applying like everyone else. Try these 8 unconventional job search moves to stand out. 🚀 The old playbook of clicking "Easy Apply" 50 times a day is dead. In a highly competitive market, the candidates winning interviews are those changing their entry mechanics. If you are currently hunting for your next role, test these strategic moves: 1️⃣ Apply in targeted windows, not the midnight rush. Submitting your profile during working hours (Tuesday through Thursday morning) increases the odds that your application hits the recruiter's inbox right when they open their dashboard. Avoid the weekend pileup. 2️⃣ Use the "People Also Viewed" strategy. When you locate a relevant job posting, visit the job poster's profile. Check the "People also viewed" sidebar. This section is an automated goldmine for finding hiring managers in similar roles or at direct competitor firms. 3️⃣ Attach a 30-second video note. Don't just send text. Summarize one hyper-relevant, quantifiable career win on camera. Recruiters remember distinct faces and voices far longer than standard black-and-white resumes. 4️⃣ Ask for a "regret interview" post-rejection. When turned down, reply cleanly: "Thank you for the time. I would value a brief 10-minute feedback conversation to close my skill gaps." A small percentage will say yes, and those candid chats frequently evolve into unadvertised pipeline leads. 5️⃣ Source by skill-stacks instead of job titles. Instead of typing "Data Analyst" into the search bar, look up precise clusters: "SQL + Tableau + stakeholder reporting." Titles vary wildly across organizations, but foundational skill combos surface hidden listings. 6️⃣ Locate niche communication channels. Many high-priority contract and critical referral roles are socialized inside developer Slack workspaces, tech Discords, or specific skill communities before they ever reach mainstream job boards. 7️⃣ Interact with target companies, not just active listings. Turn on alerts for key target employers and engage constructively with their content. Internal talent acquisition teams track active engagement; it serves as a warm signal before your resume lands in their queue. 8️⃣ Send a single, professional direct message. After applying, message the recruiter exactly once: "Hi [Name], I've submitted my application for the [Role]. I was particularly drawn to your team's work on [Specific Initiative] and would love to connect." No spam, no aggressive follow-up. --- ⚠️ Strategic Reality Check: While delaying applications is sometimes suggested to avoid the day-one flood, leading industry data confirms that early applicants still capture the highest callback rates. The key isn't waiting—it’s ensuring your profile is personalized enough to stand out in the first wave. Job seekers—which of these unconventional tactics has yielded the best conversion rate for your funnel? Let’s share notes below. 👇 #JobSearchHacks #CareerAdvice #IndiaJobs #JobSeekers #RecruitmentTrends

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Craig Allan
    2w
    • Report this post

    r.i.p. the "easy apply" button. poozle just made it obsolete i've tested every job board for the past year. linkedin, indeed, glassdoor, ziprecruiter, etc... they all have the same problem: by the time you see a job, 800+ people already applied. your resume is dead on arrival. then i built poozle and the math changed completely. poozle doesn't show you "more jobs." it pre-qualifies you for roles where you're actually 1 of 30, not 1 of 800. here's what makes it different: • pre-qualification matching: we analyze your background and only show you jobs where you're statistically likely to land an interview • 50-applicant cap: we stop showing roles once they hit 50 applicants so you're never 1 of 800 • swipe to apply: find a match - swipe right. not interested - swipe left. applying takes 2 seconds, not 15 minutes • fresh jobs only: we index directly from company career pages so you see roles in the first 0-72 hours, not week-old postings this is a battle-tested system that: • gets you in front of hiring managers before the flood hits • keeps you in manageable applicant pools where you can actually be seen • eliminates the spreadsheet nightmare (we track everything for you) job seekers are landing interviews 4x faster because they stopped applying to black holes. here's what the difference looks like: applying on linkedin: • find a job with 847 applicants • spend 30 minutes tailoring your resume • submit into the void • get auto-rejected or ghosted • repeat the cycle applying on poozle: • open the app • see each pre-qualified match • swipe right on the ones that fit • you're applicant number 12, 8, and 23 • get interview requests within a week same resume. same skills. 30x better odds. the problem isn't you. it's the pool you're swimming in. i'm giving away free poozle pro access to the next 100 people. if you're tired of the linkedin black hole, this is your exit. download Poozle free: iOS: 👉 https://lnkd.in/exzGsYFX Android: 👉 https://lnkd.in/ec2232up like this post + comment "POOZLE" for a free month of pro. (must be connected so i can DM you) p.s. repost for priority, inbox gets busy fast! p.p.s. if you've applied to 100+ jobs with zero interviews, it's not a resume problem. it's a targeting problem. poozle fixes that.

    20 11 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Zoyeb Batliwala
    3w Edited
    • Report this post

    Behind-the-scenes job search truths: 1. Jobs expire on portals, but the hiring process continues. 2. Most job portals run auto scripts and crawl from the main source, i.e., career pages and ATS platforms. 3. One portal will scrape from another, which is why you will keep clicking apply, apply, apply on the same job across different job portals. 4. Unknown and less popular job portals don't often expire old listings. 5. Recruiters may post the same job with different titles and codes, sometimes because purchased job slots may be expiring or lying unused. 6. Even after an offer is made, negotiations fail, notice periods clash, requirements change, a select few get retained, offer shopping happens, life changes are real, and a job can reopen. 7. Only the Hiring Manager and/or Recruiter can tell you whether a role is truly filled. --- So what should you do? 1. Follow company pages directly. 2. Track career pages and ATS links of your target companies in a Google Sheet. 3. You can make your personal company page on LinkedIn called "My Jobs" and follow company pages from that page to generate a clean feed of posts only from your target companies. 4. Follow and connect with the recruiters and hiring managers directly from your target companies. 5. Buy LinkedIn Premium Business only for the purpose of including a custom message when sending a connection request. 6. Find people already working in the same job titles from the People tab of each company page. They may be on hiring panels or may review applications with the recruiter. 7. Find their official work email IDs through LinkedIn contact info, company websites, and email-finder tools. 8. Search on Google for their office email ID using their name, company name, and LinkedIn. 9. Like and comment meaningfully on their posts. --- Hi, I’m Zoyeb Batliwala. 👋 I have 10 years of recruitment experience, and I help people improve their resumes, LinkedIn profiles, job search strategy, recruiter outreach, and interview preparation. Basic job-search guidance is free. Resume revisions, additions, and corrections start at ₹200. You can WhatsApp me at wa.me/919321234719, email me at zoyebbatliwala@proton.me, or follow/connect with me on LinkedIn.

    4 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • WahResume

    256 followers

    3w
    • Report this post

    Treat job search like product development, and you'll triple your ATS pass rate in one week. Most people spray and pray with the same resume across 50 applications. Zero iteration. It feels efficient but gets zero results — generic resumes pass ATS screens just 20-30% of the time. A 2024 Jobscan analysis of thousands of ATS outcomes showed applicants who tailored keywords to hit 75-80% alignment with each job description were 3x more likely to advance. We test this weekly with clients: track pass rates, tweak, repeat. Here's the 4-step loop: 1. Run your resume through Jobscan or Resonizer against 3 target JDs. Note the match score. 2. Pull top 8-10 keywords from the JD (skills, tools, metrics they emphasize). Before: "Led marketing campaigns" -> After: "Led SEO-optimized campaigns using Google Analytics and HubSpot that boosted organic traffic 45%" 3. Rewrite 4-6 bullets to weave in those keywords naturally. Aim for 75%+ match. 4. Re-scan and apply to 10 jobs. Log pass rates. Next week, iterate on failures. One client went from 2/20 passes to 14/20 in 7 days. Same resume, weekly tweaks. What's your current ATS match score on a target job? Try step 1 and report back. #WahResume #JobSearch #CareerTips

    1
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

    LinkedIn automates job application process for premium users: The platform's Premium Apply Assistant will use artificial intelligence to pre-fill applications and even add cover letters for suitable roles. https://bit.ly/4xJpLsB

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Mohd Kaleem
    3w
    • Report this post

    Your resume isn't getting rejected because you're underqualified. It's how you're describing your work. 📄 Recruiters spend about 6-7 seconds scanning a resume on first pass. A vague bullet like "managed social media accounts" gets skipped. A specific one doesn't. The numbers back this up: → 34% of hiring managers pass over resumes with few or no measurable results → 58% of recruiters say quantifiable achievements are the most persuasive thing on a resume → Resumes with measurable results get 40% more callbacks than those without → Less than 20% of applications include any specific, measurable achievements That last stat is the real opportunity. If you're the 1 in 5 who shows real numbers, you're not competing with everyone else — you're in a different category. Here's the formula that fixes most weak resumes: Action + Impact + Metric ✅ [What you did] + [Why it mattered] + [What changed because of it] See it in action: 📱 Marketing Before: "Managed social media accounts." After: "Drove 42% Instagram engagement growth in 3 months with short-form content." 💻 Software Developer Before: "Worked on web application development." After: "Built & deployed a customer self-service dashboard, reducing support dependency by 28%." 🎨 UI/UX Designer Before: "Designed website pages." After: "Enhanced onboarding flow, increasing user activation and signup completion by 23%." 📊 Data Analyst Before: "Created reports and dashboards." After: "Automated weekly reporting, cutting reporting time by 94%." 💰 Sales Executive Before: "Handled client relationships and sales." After: "Prospected & closed new business, generating $2M in quarterly sales revenue." 🧑💼 Recruiter/HR Before: "Managed recruitment process." After: "Optimized recruitment processes, cutting average time-to-hire from 45 to 28 days." 🚀 Product Manager Before: "Worked with teams to launch features." After: "Led the launch of a user-requested feature, driving a 17% increase in active feature usage." 💼 Accountant Before: "Managed company finances and reports." After: "Implemented automated reconciliation processes, reducing month-end closing time by 35%." Responsibilities tell recruiters what your job was. Achievements tell recruiters why they should hire YOU specifically. If you're thinking "I don't have numbers like that" — almost everyone does, you just haven't gone looking yet. Ask yourself for each role: → Did I make something faster, cheaper, or easier? ⚡ → Did I handle a certain volume — customers, projects, transactions? 🔢 → Did a number go up or down because of something I did? 📈📉 → Did I manage a budget, team size, or timeline? 👥 Even an estimate beats nothing. "Reduced processing time by roughly 30%" is far stronger than "Improved processing efficiency." Tonight, pick your three weakest bullet points and rewrite each one using Action + Impact + Metric. That one exercise could be the difference between your resume sitting in a pile and landing an interview. 🎯

    3
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Venkata Naga Sai Kumar Bysani
    2w
    • Report this post

    Stop applying to jobs manually in 2026. These 5 websites will save you hours every week. (Save this!) 𝟏. Teal  ↳ Scores your resume against job descriptions with keyword matching  ↳ Used by 4M+ professionals to land interviews 6x faster ↳ Built-in job tracker + 40 email templates for follow-ups  → https://tealhq.co/4fcjgrj 𝟐. JobCopilot ↳ Auto-applies to up to 50 jobs per day while you sleep  ↳ Scans 500,000+ company career pages daily for new openings  → https://lnkd.in/dEVsCgSA 𝟑. Jobright.ai ↳ AI matches you to jobs from an 8M+ job database refreshed with 400K+ new listings daily ↳ Users report 3x more interviews and 80% time saved → https://lnkd.in/dB-jxX_z 𝟒. Simplify ↳ One-click autofill for job applications (free Chrome extension)  ↳ 300M+ applications submitted, used by 1.8M+ candidates → https://simplify.jobs 𝟓. 𝐇𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫.𝐢𝐨 ↳ Find recruiter and hiring manager emails for direct outreach  ↳ Free tier available with 25 searches + 50 verifications per month  → https://hunter.io 𝐌𝐲 𝟐 𝐜𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬:  → Use Teal to tailor your resume for roles you really want → Use JobCopilot or Jobright for volume  → Use Hunter to reach out directly when you find your dream job 𝐏𝐫𝐨 𝐓𝐢𝐩: Don't rely on just one tool. Stack them. Tailor for quality, auto-apply for volume, and reach out directly to stand out. The job market is brutal right now. Work smarter, not harder. Which of these have you tried? Any others I should add? 👇 ♻️ Repost to help someone stuck in application hell
